raw_case:
A Rare Case of Malignant Melanoma of the Glans Penis in a Young Male Treated with an Organ-Preserving Surgical Procedure
Penile melanoma is a rare and highly invasive cancer that is generally diagnosed in the sixth to seventh decades of life. We report a rare case of primary malignant melanoma of the glans penis in a 38-year-old man who presented with a proliferative lesion over the glans with discoloration for six months. The clinical and metastatic workups were unremarkable. Circumcision and biopsy performed outside showed characteristics of squamous cell carcinoma. We treated the patient with a partial penectomy, and the histopathological examination was suggestive of malignant melanoma. A stump revision with bilateral superficial inguinal lymph node dissection was performed. As per the oncologist's opinion, the patient underwent chemotherapy and immunotherapy after surgery. No recurrence or metastasis occurred during the one-year follow-up.

Introduction

Penile melanoma (PM), comprising less than 2% of penile cancers and 0.1% of all melanomas [1], predominantly occurs on the glans penis (55%), prepuce (28%), shaft (9%), and meatus (8%) [2], with a mean patient age of 52.9 years [3]. Since the first documented case in 1859, only 220 cases have been reported globally [4]. PM originates from the malignant transformation of melanocytes in the neuroectodermal layer, with PMs classified as cutaneous or mucosal based on their location [1,5]. Staging follows a three-stage or American Joint Committee on Cancer tumor, node, metastasis (AJCC TNM) staging system [3,6,7]. This case report presents a young male with malignant melanoma of the glans penis involving the urethral meatus and reviews the literature on the pathogenesis and treatment of PM.

Case presentation

A 38-year-old Indian man visited the Urology Department with a chief complaint of lesions on his glans penis for the past six months. The lesion was painless, and there were no associated complaints. No urinary or sexual complaints were reported. The patient is sexually active. The patient had no history of severe illness, comorbidities, allergy, or trauma to the penis. The family history was also insignificant. He worked as a laborer and had a low socio-economic level. The patient underwent circumcision with a biopsy from the lesion at an outside hospital, and the biopsy revealed squamous cell carcinoma. The patient's clinical examination was unremarkable, and local examination revealed proliferative lesions with irregular margins present over the left lateral aspect of the glans penis around the urethral meatus, with dark brown discoloration. The lesion was non-tender and firm in consistency, and no active discharge was observed (Figure 1). No palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy was noted. Testis, scrotum, and prostate examinations were unremarkable.All routine blood and urine investigations were within normal limits. The contrast-enhanced computed tomography scan of the head, chest, and abdomen did not show evidence of metastatic disease. Subsequently, the patient underwent partial penectomy (Figure 2) based on an outside biopsy report.Pathological sections of the partial penectomy specimen showed malignant melanoma involving the urethral lining and involvement of the proximal urethral margin (Figures 3-4).Immunohistochemistry (IHC) markers of these specimens were positive for human melanoma black (HMB-45) (Figure 5), negative for pancytokeratin (Figure 6), and positive for S-100 (Figure 7).Since the proximal urethral margin was positive in partial penectomy specimens, and because of the aggressive nature of malignant melanoma histopathology, the patient was subjected to revision of the stump of the penis with bilateral superficial inguinal lymph node dissection (Figure 8).The final histopathology report showed malignant melanoma with a proximal resected margin, and bilateral inguinal lymph node specimens were negative and tumor-free (stage I organ-confined).After seeking an oncologist's opinion, the patient was started on a course of chemotherapy with cisplatin, bleomycin, vincristine, and thymosin immunotherapy. The patient does not show any recurrence or metastasis at the one-year follow-up.

Discussion

PM, a rare cancer, accounts for under 0.2% of all male melanomas [2,8], predominantly occurring on the glans penis (82%), then the prepuce, urethral meatus, and shaft [8], peaking in the sixth to seventh decades [9,10]. Its advanced stages can cause dysuria, obstruction, hematuria, discharge, and sometimes melanic and fistula formation. Diagnosis is frequently delayed due to patient hesitation and diagnostic challenges [2,8]. It usually appears as a 1 cm pigmented papule, plaque, or ulceration, with differential diagnoses including junctional melanocytic nevus, penile melanosis, lentigo, and atypical pigmented macules [11]. These benign conditions are clinically similar to MM, making biopsy and histologic analysis essential for accurate diagnosis and avoiding excessive surgery. MM staging comprises stage I (limited to the penis), stage II (affects regional lymph nodes), and stage III (widespread) [12], with 43-60% of cases showing lymph node involvement at diagnosis [1-4]. The best treatment strategy is still debated, with some favoring aggressive surgical interventions [13].Literature advocates for conservative penile surgery (local excision with a 3 to 5 cm margin or distal partial penectomy) when inguinal nodes are nonpalpable in patients with thin lesions (<1.5 mm) and recommend prophylactic superficial inguinal node dissection for lesions thicker than 1.5 mm3 [14,15]. Alternatively, sentinel lymphadenectomy using radiocolloid mapping and dye localization can avoid the morbidity of bilateral superficial inguinal node dissections while providing accurate staging for treatment and prognosis [15,16]. Chemotherapy is indicated for disseminated melanoma, with combination chemotherapy (Dacarbazine (DTIC), bis-chloroethyl nitrosourea (BCNU), cisplatin, and tamoxifen) yielding response rates of 15% to 45% [14-16].Some literature noted that local excision or partial penile amputation with an adequate margin is effective for controlling T1 and T2 PMs. However, patients with proven metastases often succumb despite surgery and chemotherapy. Early-stage aggressive disease may be curable (Evidence Level 4) [17]. Literature also reported higher survival rates for patients undergoing wide local excision. Still, the prognosis remains poor due to delayed diagnoses and advanced disease characterized by suspicious inguinal crural adenopathy (Evidence Level 4) [18]. The patient prognosis for PM depends on the primary tumor stage and inguinal metastases presence [19]. Studies suggest that two-year and five-year survival rates are at 63% and 31%, respectively. All patients with nodal or distant metastases at diagnosis died within two years. The prognosis worsened significantly with ulceration, tumor depth ≥ 3.5 mm, or tumor diameter > 15 mm.

Conclusions

PMs are rare but highly treatable with early surgical excision, given their resistance to chemotherapy and radiotherapy. Delays in diagnosis and surgery can worsen prognosis. Anamnesis, physical examination, and imaging studies are essential for improving patient survival. This case report underscores the effectiveness of conservative surgical treatment following recent guidelines, incorporating a 1 cm margin to prevent recurrence. Early detection is crucial for effective treatment in our patients. Early recognition, aggressive surgical intervention, and effective adjuvant therapy are vital for improving prognosis while preserving penile aesthetics and function.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 38-year-old Indian male, laborer, low socio-economic level, sexually active.
- Chief Complaint: Painless lesion on the glans penis for six months.
- History of Present Illness: Lesion over the glans penis with discoloration for six months, no urinary or sexual complaints.
- Past Medical History: None.
- Personal History: No history of severe illness, comorbidities, allergies, or trauma to the penis.
- Family History: None.
- Physical Examination: Proliferative lesion with dark brown discoloration and irregular margins on the left lateral aspect of the glans penis involving the urethral meatus; non-tender, firm consistency, no active discharge, no palpable inguinal lymphadenopathy; normal testis, scrotum, and prostate examinations.
- Ancillary Tests: Routine blood and urine tests within normal limits; contrast-enhanced CT scan of the head, chest, and abdomen showed no metastatic disease. Histopathological biopsy initially suggested squamous cell carcinoma. Immunohistochemistry showed positivity for HMB-45 and S-100 (melanoma markers), negative for pancytokeratin, confirming malignant melanoma. Lymph node biopsies were negative for tumor involvement.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Diagnosis of Squamous Cell Carcinoma**: Based on the initial biopsy report from an outside hospital, the lesion was presumed to be squamous cell carcinoma. This was reasonable, given the lesion's appearance and commonality of this diagnosis in penile dermatologic disorders. However, further investigation via partial penectomy and immunohistochemical analysis showed melanoma markers (HMB-45 and S-100) and ruled out squamous cell carcinoma (negative pancytokeratin stain).
   
2. **Consideration of Other Pigmented Lesions**: Differential diagnoses included benign conditions such as penile melanosis, lentigo, or atypical pigmented macules. The firm consistency and discoloration, coupled with histological evidence of malignant melanoma, excluded benign conditions.

3. **Confirmation of Penile Melanoma**: Based on histopathological findings of malignant melanoma and immunohistochemical markers, a definitive diagnosis was established. Imaging tests ruled out metastasis, supporting an early-stage (stage I) diagnosis. Proximal urethral margin involvement warranted stump revision with bilateral superficial inguinal lymph node dissection, which confirmed absence of lymph node involvement.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Malignant melanoma of the glans penis (stage I, organ-confined).
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Malignant melanoma of the glans penis (stage I).

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start with this case. The patient is a 38-year-old Indian male, a laborer with low socio-economic status and is sexually active. The main complaint is a painless lesion on the glans penis that's been there for six months. No urinary or sexual issues mentioned. Past medical history is clear, no comorbidities or allergies. Family history is also negative.

Physical exam shows a proliferative lesion with dark brown discoloration and irregular margins on the left side of the glans, involving the urethral meatus. It's non-tender, firm, no discharge, and no inguinal lymph nodes. Testes, scrotum, and prostate are normal. The blood and urine tests are normal. CT scans of head, chest, and abdomen don't show metastases. The initial biopsy suggested squamous cell carcinoma, but immunohistochemistry showed HMB-45 and S-100 positive (melanoma markers), and negative for pancytokeratin. Lymph nodes are negative for tumor.

First, I need to consider the differential diagnoses for a penile lesion. The key points here are the location, duration, appearance, and the biopsy results. Since it's painless and has been present for six months, it's not acute. The dark brown color and irregular margins might suggest a melanoma, but I should think about other possibilities first.

The initial biopsy was squamous cell carcinoma, but the immunohistochemistry changed that. Melanoma markers being positive (HMB-45, S-100) and negative for keratin (which is more for epithelial cells) points away from squamous cell carcinoma. Melanoma is a possibility here. Penile melanoma is rare, but it's possible, especially in areas with chronic irritation or other risk factors. The patient's socio-economic status and occupation might relate to possible risk factors, but I'm not sure. Maybe he has a history of sun exposure? Not mentioned here.

The absence of lymphadenopathy and negative lymph node biopsies is a good sign, but doesn't rule out melanoma. The CT scans didn't show metastases, which is consistent with early-stage disease. However, melanoma can be aggressive, so the staging is important. The lesion's location on the glans, which is a common site for penile melanoma, supports the diagnosis.

Other differentials: maybe a benign melanocytic lesion like a melanoma in situ or a benign nevus. But the presence of irregular margins and the biopsy results pointing to malignancy with melanoma markers would lean towards melanoma. Also, the fact that the lesion has been growing over six months (proliferative) suggests a more aggressive process.

I need to confirm that the immunohistochemistry is correctly interpreted. HMB-45 and S-100 are typically positive in melanoma. Pancytokeratin is negative, which helps differentiate from squamous cell carcinoma. So the biopsy results are pretty conclusive here. The diagnosis would be malignant melanoma of the penis. The staging would be based on the depth of invasion, but since the lymph nodes are negative and no distant metastases, it might be stage I or II. But the user just asked for the final diagnosis, not staging.

So putting it all together, the answer should be malignant melanoma of the penis.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a painless, proliferative lesion on the glans penis with dark brown discoloration and irregular margins, which raises suspicion for a melanocytic lesion. The absence of pain, discharge, or systemic symptoms aligns with a chronic, localized process rather than an acute infection or inflammatory condition.  
<step 2> The initial histopathological diagnosis of squamous cell carcinoma was contradicted by immunohistochemistry results showing positivity for HMB-45 and S-100 (markers specific to melanoma) and negativity for pancytokeratin (a marker for epithelial cells), which strongly refutes a squamous origin.  
<step 3> The clinical and histopathological findings are consistent with melanoma, a rare but aggressive malignancy that can occur on the penis. The location on the glans, a common site for penile melanoma, and the absence of lymphadenopathy or metastatic disease on imaging further support this diagnosis.  
<step 4> The patient’s socio-economic status and occupation do not directly influence the diagnosis, but the lack of comorbidities, allergies, or prior trauma simplifies the differential to a primary cutaneous malignancy. The negative lymph node biopsies and normal imaging suggest early-stage disease.  

### Answer:
Malignant melanoma of the penis.
```
